findings.description: _is_expired returns 'age < self.ttl', the exact inversion of expiry, so fresh entries are deleted as 'expired' and stale entries are served as 'fresh' — the TTL does literally the opposite of its one job.
get() trusts the inverted _is_expired, so it evicts brand-new L1 entries and returns expired ones, making the L1 tier actively wrong rather than merely useless.
pickle.loads() on bytes fetched from Redis is arbitrary-code-execution on deserialization if anyone can write to that Redis key — a delightful RCE backdoor wearing a cache costume.
_local has no size bound or eviction, and the inverted expiry means fresh entries are never aged out on read, so the 'cache' is an unbounded memory leak that grows until OOM.
set() writes L1 then L2 non-atomically with no error handling, so if redis.setex raises the local dict and Redis silently diverge, serving stale L1 data indefinitely.

An L1 entry deleted as 'expired' then re-fetched from L2 still increments stats.hits, so the hit/miss accounting double-counts and overstates effectiveness, which is bold for a cache that doesn't work.
_is_expired is named for a truth value it computes backwards; the method name actively lies about its return semantics.
